objective: Reinforce accurate recognition of ACLS rhythms
Practice timely pharmacologic and electrical interventions
Develop team leader communication and decision-making skills under pressure
equipment: Cardiac monitor/defibrillator (functional, with rhythm generator or pre-programmed strips)
IV/IO training equipment with med syringes or mock drug kit
Airway adjuncts (BVM, OPA/NPA, advanced airway optional)
Stopwatch/timer

roles.responsibility: Recognizes rhythm, announces treatment plan, directs crew
Prepares and administers drugs at Team Lead command
Applies pads, interprets rhythm with Team Lead, performs electrical therapy as directed

student_instructions: Team Lead is responsible for recognizing rhythm, announcing treatment plan, and directing crew
Med provider prepares and administers drugs at the command of the Team Lead
Monitor operator applies pads, interprets rhythm with Team Lead, and performs electrical therapy as directed
Scenario runs in short cycles (3-5 min) per rhythm case
Rotate Team Lead role each round to maximize repetitions

briefing.script: Focus on speed, accuracy, and communication. You'll rotate as Team Lead, and I'll stop you if I need to clarify reasoning.

during_scenario: Prompt Team Lead if indecisive: 'What's your next step?'
If Team Lead hesitates >15 sec, add stress (alarms, bystander, nurse asking for orders)
rotation_instructions: Run 2 rhythm cases per Team Lead
Encourage speed but do not allow skipping safety steps (e.g., 'All clear!' before shock)

rhythm_cases.stable_presentation: Adenosine sequence: 6mg rapid IV push → 12mg → 12mg. Flush with 20mL NS via stopcock. If converts, monitor.
rhythm_cases.unstable_presentation: Synchronized cardioversion. Sedate if time allows. Start at 50-100J.
rhythm_cases.decision_point: Stability assessment determines electrical vs pharmacologic pathway

rhythm_cases.treatment_sequence: Atropine 1mg IV q3-5 min (max 3mg) → Transcutaneous pacing → Dopamine 5-20 mcg/kg/min or Epinephrine 2-10 mcg/min infusion
rhythm_cases.decision_point: Atropine vs immediate pacing depends on severity of symptoms and response

rhythm_cases.treatment_sequence: Defibrillation (200J biphasic) → CPR 2 min → Rhythm check → Epi 1mg q3-5 min → 2nd shock → Amiodarone 300mg → 3rd shock → Amiodarone 150mg
rhythm_cases.decision_point: Timing of epi vs amiodarone in the cycle; CPR quality metrics

rhythm_cases.treatment_sequence: Magnesium sulfate 1-2g IV over 5-20 min (or faster if pulseless) → Defibrillation if pulseless → Avoid amiodarone (may worsen QT prolongation)
rhythm_cases.decision_point: Recognizing Torsades vs standard VT — treatment differs significantly

key_observations: Rhythm identified correctly and announced out loud
Correct intervention chosen (pharmacologic vs electrical)
Timeliness (within ACLS standards)
Clear communication and delegation
common_errors: Delay in rhythm recognition
Wrong drug/dose sequence
Failing to confirm rhythm before intervention
Poor delegation ('I'll do everything myself')

stress_layers.description: Add artifact to monitor — requires recheck before shocking
Partner offers wrong medication — Team Lead must correct
Role player shouting, adding stress and distraction
If >15 sec hesitation, add alarms, bystander, or nurse asking for orders

walk_through.instructor_role: Active coach. Prompts: 'What rhythm is this? What's your next step? Why?' Corrects errors immediately.
walk_through.student_expectation: Verbalize every decision before acting. No time pressure.
walk_through.rhythm_complexity: Core rhythms only — NSR, VFib, VT, bradycardia, SVT. Clear presentations.
walk_through.pass_criteria: Demonstrates correct algorithm knowledge when walked through.

pressured.instructor_role: Introduces stressors but still intervenes on critical safety errors.
pressured.student_expectation: Manage distractions while maintaining algorithm fidelity. Time constraints active.
pressured.rhythm_complexity: Core + intermediate rhythms. May include artifact or ambiguous presentations.
pressured.pass_criteria: Maintains algorithm fidelity under pressure with acceptable error rate.

full_lead.instructor_role: Observer/FTO. Does not intervene unless patient safety issue.
full_lead.student_expectation: Run the code independently. Direct team, identify rhythms, order meds without prompting.
full_lead.rhythm_complexity: Full rhythm library. May include rhythm changes mid-scenario.
full_lead.pass_criteria: Independently leads code to completion with correct decisions and team direction.

objective: Build rapid decision-making skills under time pressure
Practice prioritizing interventions as the team lead
Strengthen verbalization of reasoning and treatment plans
equipment: Chairs (students in a hot seat rotation)
Whiteboard, projector, or printed case cards
Stopwatch/timer
List of short case vignettes
student_instructions: One student sits in the Hot Seat as Team Lead
Instructor presents a case vignette (1-2 sentences, vitals included)
Student has 30-60 seconds to announce: initial impression (sick/not sick), first 1-2 interventions, next steps or transport decision
Instructor may ask 1-2 follow-up questions
Rotate every 1-2 minutes to keep pace; all students should cycle through multiple cases

briefing.script: You're in the hot seat. Fast thinking, not perfect answers. Prioritize and speak your plan.

during_case: Enforce time limits — if >1 min, patient 'worsens'
If vague response, press: 'What's your NEXT SPECIFIC step?'
If wrong answer, allow learning moment: 'How would your FTO redirect you here?'

case_bank.vignette: 67M, right-sided weakness, slurred speech, onset 45 min ago, BP 210/110.

expected_response.transport_decision: Rapid transport to comprehensive stroke center with pre-notification

follow_up_questions: Why not treat the BP?
What's the difference between a primary and comprehensive stroke center?

case_bank.vignette: 18F, postictal, SpO2 85%, unresponsive, GCS 7.

expected_response.sick_not_sick: Sick — airway and respiratory compromise in postictal state
priority_interventions: High-flow O2 via NRB or BVM assist
expected_response.transport_decision: Transport with ongoing airway monitoring; prepare for recurrent seizure

follow_up_questions: What if she seizes again en route?
BGL is 42 — what changes?

case_bank.vignette: 54M, crushing chest pain, 12-lead shows anterior STEMI, BP 80/50.
follow_up_questions: Why no nitroglycerin?
BP drops to 60/palp after 500mL NS — now what?

case_bank.vignette: 24M, entrapped in MVC, GCS 7, BP 80 palp, SpO2 90%.

expected_response.sick_not_sick: Sick — multi-system trauma with hemorrhagic shock and altered mental status

follow_up_questions: Extrication will take 20 more minutes — what do you do on scene?
Breath sounds diminished on the right — what now?

case_bank.vignette: 72F with CHF history, pink frothy sputum, SpO2 70%, tripod position.

expected_response.sick_not_sick: Sick — acute pulmonary edema / decompensated CHF
priority_interventions: IV access — do NOT fluid bolus
expected_response.transport_decision: Emergent transport; position of comfort (sitting upright)

follow_up_questions: Why not fluid bolus?
SpO2 still 78% after CPAP — what's next?

case_bank.vignette: 6-year-old, stridor at rest, febrile, drooling.

expected_response.sick_not_sick: Sick — upper airway obstruction, likely epiglottitis
priority_interventions: Do NOT examine throat or lay supine
Prepare advanced airway equipment as backup only
expected_response.transport_decision: Rapid transport — let the ED/OR manage the airway in a controlled environment

follow_up_questions: Child becomes completely obstructed — what now?
Parents are refusing transport — how do you handle this?

case_bank.vignette: 32F, sudden onset facial swelling, urticaria, and wheezing after eating shellfish at a restaurant. BP 78/42, HR 130, SpO2 88%, GCS 15.

expected_response.sick_not_sick: Sick — hypotension + respiratory compromise = severe anaphylaxis
priority_interventions: Aggressive IV fluid bolus (NS wide open)
Prepare for advanced airway if swelling worsens
Diphenhydramine/famotidine as adjuncts (NOT substitutes for epi)
expected_response.transport_decision: Emergent transport; repeat epi q5-15 min if no improvement

follow_up_questions: What if the patient's airway swells shut after your first dose of epi?
Patient improves but BP drops again 20 minutes later — what now?
case_bank.instructor_tip: Students often give diphenhydramine first — redirect to epinephrine as the only first-line treatment.

case_bank.vignette: 45M, found confused and diaphoretic by coworkers. Known diabetic on insulin. BGL 28 mg/dL, HR 110, BP 138/80, GCS 10 (E3 V3 M4).

expected_response.sick_not_sick: Sick — severely altered mental status with critically low BGL
priority_interventions: IV access → D10 or D50 (per protocol) titrated to mental status
Reassess BGL q5 min; repeat dextrose if needed
Airway positioning and suction standby (aspiration risk)
expected_response.transport_decision: Transport for evaluation; search for underlying cause

follow_up_questions: You can't get IV access — what's your backup plan?
BGL comes up to 110 but patient refuses transport — how do you counsel them?
case_bank.instructor_tip: Common freeze point is when IV access fails. Prompt for IM glucagon.

case_bank.vignette: 22M, found unresponsive in a parking lot. Pinpoint pupils, RR 4, SpO2 72%, HR 58, BP 90/60. Track marks noted on arms.

expected_response.sick_not_sick: Sick — respiratory failure from suspected opioid overdose
priority_interventions: Naloxone 0.4-2mg IN/IV/IM — titrate to adequate respirations, NOT full consciousness
Prepare for vomiting/agitation on waking — recovery position, suction ready
expected_response.transport_decision: Transport; consider polysubstance if poor response to naloxone

follow_up_questions: You give 2mg naloxone IN and no response after 3 minutes — next steps?
Patient wakes up combative and wants to leave — what are your concerns?
case_bank.instructor_tip: Watch for students who over-dose naloxone. Emphasize titrating to respiratory effort, not alertness.

case_bank.vignette: 58M, collapsed at a gym, bystander CPR in progress. AED applied, advises shock. No pulse confirmed.

priority_interventions: ETCO2 monitoring for CPR quality and ROSC detection
expected_response.transport_decision: Work on scene unless specific transport criteria met; identify reversible causes (H's and T's)

follow_up_questions: After 3 shocks and 2 rounds of epi, patient is now in PEA — what changes?
You get ROSC — what are your immediate priorities?
case_bank.instructor_tip: Push for verbalization of CPR quality metrics (rate, depth, recoil, fraction).

case_bank.vignette: 30F, pulled from a house fire. Singed nasal hairs, hoarse voice, circumferential burns to both arms and anterior chest. HR 128, BP 100/60, SpO2 94%, RR 28.

expected_response.sick_not_sick: Sick — inhalation injury signs + significant BSA burns (~27% by Rule of 9's)
priority_interventions: Early intubation — hoarseness and singed nasal hairs indicate impending airway compromise
High-flow O2 via NRB (CO poisoning risk)
IV fluid resuscitation: Parkland formula (4mL × kg × %BSA), half in first 8 hrs

follow_up_questions: Patient's voice is getting progressively weaker — how urgent is your airway intervention?
What if your transport time to the burn center is 45 minutes?
case_bank.instructor_tip: Students frequently underestimate inhalation injury. The airway question should come before burn care.

case_bank.vignette: 78F, nursing home patient, fever 103.2°F, altered from baseline. HR 126, BP 82/48, RR 26, SpO2 91%, skin mottled and cool. Foley catheter in place.

expected_response.sick_not_sick: Sick — meets sepsis criteria (suspected infection + hypotension + AMS + tachycardia)
priority_interventions: Aggressive IV NS fluid bolus (30 mL/kg initial)
If hypotension persists after fluids: push-dose epi or norepinephrine drip per protocol
expected_response.transport_decision: Early hospital notification — sepsis alert; rapid transport

follow_up_questions: You've given 2 liters of NS and BP is still 76/40 — what now?
What's your likely source of infection here?
case_bank.instructor_tip: Many students miss aggressive fluid resuscitation as a prehospital intervention. This is a volume problem first.

case_bank.vignette: 28M, found running in traffic, screaming incoherently. Tased by PD with no effect. Profusely diaphoretic, superhuman strength reported. HR 188, temp 105.4°F.

expected_response.sick_not_sick: Sick — excited delirium is a life-threatening emergency (high cardiac arrest risk)
priority_interventions: Scene safety paramount — coordinate with law enforcement
Chemical sedation early: ketamine IM (4 mg/kg) or midazolam per protocol
Once safe: cardiac monitor, SpO2, BGL, 12-lead
Active cooling measures (ice packs to groin/axillae, cold saline if available)
expected_response.transport_decision: Rapid transport; continuous monitoring — high risk for sudden cardiac arrest

follow_up_questions: After sedation the patient goes into cardiac arrest — what's your differential?
How does your approach change if ketamine isn't in your formulary?
case_bank.instructor_tip: Scene safety and early sedation are critical — students may try to assess before the scene is controlled.

case_bank.vignette: 28F, 37 weeks pregnant, reports her water broke and feels something between her legs. Umbilical cord visible at introitus. FHR by doppler: 80 bpm (bradycardic).
case_bank.vitals: Mother: HR 108, BP 118/72, RR 22, SpO2 98%. FHR 80 bpm

expected_response.sick_not_sick: Sick — true obstetric emergency with immediate threat to fetal life
priority_interventions: Do NOT push cord back in
Keep cord moist with warm saline-soaked gauze
Gloved hand in vagina to elevate presenting part off cord (relieve compression)
High-flow O2 to mother, IV fluid bolus
expected_response.transport_decision: Emergent transport — notify hospital for immediate C-section. Do NOT delay on scene.

follow_up_questions: FHR drops to 60 during transport — are you still holding pressure off the cord?
What if the mother starts pushing involuntarily?
case_bank.instructor_tip: High-stress, low-frequency call. Students may not remember the hand-in-vagina maneuver — coach through it.

key_observations: Clear sick/not sick declaration
Immediate life threat addressed first
Logical sequencing (e.g., airway before IV)
Confidence and communication under pressure
common_errors: Freezing or overthinking
Overcomplicating treatment instead of addressing basics
Forgetting transport/scene time considerations

stress_layers.description: Only 15 seconds to answer
Student in hot seat must delegate tasks to 2 crew members
Change vitals mid-answer (BP drops, LOC worsens)
Background noise or radio interruptions
Role-player asking irrelevant questions ('Can I ride in the ambulance?')

objective: Develop adaptability when equipment, time, or personnel are limited
Practice prioritization of interventions and patient safety under pressure
Reinforce leadership and delegation skills when resources are constrained
equipment: Manikin or role player (medical or trauma case)
Partial equipment setup (deliberately leave items missing or broken)
2-3 students per group: Team Lead + Partner(s)
Instructor sheet with case details and resource restrictions
student_instructions: Team Lead receives case presentation
Instructor announces a limitation (e.g., 'You only have one oxygen source,' 'Your IV kit is missing catheters')
Students must manage patient with available resources, directing team and adapting plan
Case runs for 5-7 minutes, then rotate Team Lead
Quick feedback provided before next run

briefing.script: You don't always get what you want in the field. Today you'll have to adapt, prioritize, and make the best clinical decisions with what you've got.

during_scenario: Ensure Team Lead gives clear delegation (not just 'I'll do it all')
Prompt if they freeze: 'What's your backup if you don't have X?'
Push realism — FTOs will expect resourcefulness, not excuses

case_bank.vignette: Hypotensive, febrile patient — but IV supplies limited to one catheter and one bag of NS.
case_bank.resource_restriction: Only 1 IV catheter and 1 liter NS available
case_bank.expected_adaptation: Prioritize single successful IV attempt. If missed: IO access. Conserve fluid — run wide open but be aware of limited supply. Request mutual aid for additional supplies. Focus on O2, transport, early notification.
case_bank.life_threat_priority: Hypotension and sepsis — fluid resuscitation is critical even with limited supply

case_bank.vignette: Severe respiratory distress, SpO2 70% — but only one oxygen tank available.
case_bank.resource_restriction: Single portable O2 tank, partially full
case_bank.expected_adaptation: Patient gets the O2 first (NRB or CPAP if available). Conserve flow rate if possible. Consider: can you use BVM without supplemental O2 as backup? Nitroglycerin for preload reduction. Rapid transport.
case_bank.life_threat_priority: Hypoxia — the single O2 source goes to the patient, not to power a nebulizer or other secondary use

case_bank.vignette: MVC with hypotension and hypoxia — but monitor won't power up for first 2 minutes.
case_bank.vitals: HR ~130 (manual), BP 80/palp (manual), RR 28, SpO2 unknown initially
case_bank.resource_restriction: Cardiac monitor non-functional for the first 2 minutes of patient contact
case_bank.expected_adaptation: Rely on clinical signs: manual pulse rate and quality, skin signs (pale, cool, diaphoretic), mental status, manual BP. Don't freeze waiting for the monitor. Begin interventions based on clinical assessment. Reassess when monitor becomes available.
case_bank.life_threat_priority: Hemorrhagic shock — don't delay treatment waiting for technology

case_bank.vignette: Facial swelling, wheezing — but Epi 1:1000 not in kit.
case_bank.resource_restriction: Epi 1:1000 vial missing from drug kit. Autoinjector (EpiPen) available. Epi 1:10,000 (cardiac concentration) available.
case_bank.expected_adaptation: Use autoinjector if available (0.3mg IM). If only cardiac epi (1:10,000): can draw 0.3mg (3mL) and give IM — know the concentration difference. Aggressive IV fluids. High-flow O2. Diphenhydramine as adjunct.
case_bank.life_threat_priority: Anaphylaxis — epinephrine by any available route is the priority. Don't withhold treatment because the preferred formulation isn't available.

key_observations: Does the Team Lead recognize limitations quickly?
Do they adapt safely instead of stalling?
Do they communicate changes to the team clearly?
Do they still address life threats first?
common_errors: Students freeze when expected equipment isn't available
Ignoring alternatives (e.g., IO if IV fails, BLS airway if advanced unavailable)
Wasting time trying to 'fix' problem instead of treating patient

stress_layers.description: Tank runs out, monitor dies, partner's IV infiltrates
Only one crew member available — force solo leadership
Visible timer adding time pressure
Patient worsens if not managed within 2-3 minutes
Add second minor patient needing attention — forces triage/prioritization

objective: Strengthen team leadership and closed-loop communication
Develop ability to assign tasks and manage scene flow
Reinforce that clear direction equals smoother patient care and better outcomes
equipment: One manikin or role player (moderate medical or trauma case)
2-3 crew members (students role-playing partners/EMTs)
Radio or simulated hospital patch script
Basic airway, monitor, IV/IO kit
Stopwatch/timer
student_instructions: One student is designated Team Lead. Others act as crew.
Team Lead must: assign initial roles (airway, monitor, IV, meds), direct interventions in correct order, verbally summarize findings and plan, give a brief radio report to receiving hospital
Each scenario runs 7-8 minutes
Rotate Team Lead role each round so every student practices

briefing.script: Your job is not just to know what to do, but to get others to do it. Today's focus is on communication and clear delegation.

during_scenario: Crew members may 'act confused' if instructions aren't clear
Prompt if Team Lead forgets delegation: 'Who's doing airway?'
Observe tone, clarity, and whether Team Lead reassesses/re-directs as needed

case_bank.vignette: Elderly patient, pink frothy sputum, tripod position, severe distress.
Diaphoretic, confused patient, BGL 32.
expected_delegations: Monitor: Continuous, BGL recheck q5 min

case_bank.vignette: Patient with abdominal pain, tachycardia, borderline BP.
expected_delegations: IV: Bilateral large bore, fluid bolus

key_observations: Initial role assignments — Team Lead sets direction immediately
Clear, concise instructions with names ('John, get the monitor on. Sarah, get IV access.')
Closed-loop communication ('Repeat back the med and dose before giving')
Updates to crew and hospital patch
common_errors: Vague orders ('Somebody get an IV')
Trying to do everything themselves
Not correcting partner mistakes
Forgetting hospital/radio communication

stress_layers.description: Visible countdown for interventions
Instructor interrupts: 'Hospital calling for ETA'
Rhythm change or vitals drop mid-scenario
Crew member suggests wrong intervention ('Let's just give morphine')
Mid-scenario role switch — crew member gets 'pulled away'

objective: Reinforce psychomotor skills under distraction
Develop ability to verbalize reasoning while performing tasks
Build stress tolerance for real-world interruptions (FTO questioning, radio calls, family members)
equipment: Choose one skill per round (rotate weekly): IV/IO start (arm trainer or IO trainer), Airway management (BVM, adjuncts, supraglottic, ET), Medication draw-up/administration (mock meds and syringes)
Instructor with question bank (printed cards or list)
Timer
Optional background noise (radio chatter, music)

skills_rotated.equipment: Arm trainer or IO trainer, IV start kit, IO kit
BVM, OPA/NPA, supraglottic airway, ET tube, laryngoscope, intubation head
Mock med vials, syringes, 3-way stopcock, IV tubing

student_instructions: Student begins performing the assigned skill
Instructor asks clinical reasoning and recall questions throughout
Student must answer while continuing the skill
Skill must be completed successfully within time limit (2-3 minutes)
Rotate students quickly; all should complete at least one skill attempt

briefing.script: In the field, you'll be asked questions or distracted while doing critical skills. The goal here is to keep working and think out loud.

during_scenario: Fire off 4-6 questions during the attempt
Stop if unsafe technique, otherwise allow to complete
debrief_each_student: Did they keep moving hands while answering?
Did they demonstrate resilience under stress?

pathophysiology_indications: What physiologic problem are we trying to correct with push-dose epinephrine?
Why might a patient become hypotensive after induction for RSI?
How does epinephrine improve coronary and cerebral perfusion pressure during shock?
What is the difference between distributive shock and cardiogenic shock in terms of vascular tone?
Why is push-dose epinephrine appropriate for peri-intubation hypotension but not for cardiac arrest?
pharmacodynamics_pharmacokinetics: What receptor types does epinephrine act on?
Which receptor effect predominates at lower doses? At higher doses?
How does epinephrine increase blood pressure?
What is the onset and duration of action for push-dose epinephrine?
How does the half-life of epinephrine affect your need to monitor and potentially redose?
dosing_administration: What is the concentration of push-dose epinephrine after dilution?
Walk me through the math to make 10 mcg/mL from a cardiac epi amp (1 mg/10 mL).
What is the usual dose range for push-dose epi?
Why do we dilute epinephrine before giving it IV push?
What's the maximum total dose you'd reasonably expect to give in this setting?
adverse_effects_safety: What are the major risks of giving push-dose epinephrine?
What would you do if the patient develops tachyarrhythmia after a dose?
How could dosing errors with push-dose epinephrine be dangerous?
What's the difference in effect between giving 10 mcg IV push versus accidentally giving 1 mg IV push?
How might push-dose epi affect myocardial oxygen demand?
comparisons_alternatives: How does push-dose epi compare to phenylephrine?
Why might norepinephrine be preferred for continuous infusion over push-dose epi?
If you had no epinephrine, what other medication could you use for peri-intubation hypotension?
In what patient populations might phenylephrine be a safer option than epinephrine?
scenario_based: You give a dose of push-dose epi, but the blood pressure only improves for 2 minutes before dropping again. What's your next step?
Your patient's heart rate jumps from 88 to 160 after a dose. What are your priorities?
A patient in septic shock is profoundly hypotensive despite fluids. Is push-dose epi appropriate, or do you need something else? Why?
During RSI, the BP is 60/40 after induction. You have phenylephrine and epinephrine available — how do you choose?

key_observations: Hands keep moving while answering
Clear, concise responses (not long-winded)
Accurate recall of key clinical facts
Ability to stay composed under distraction
common_errors: Freezing or stopping the skill to answer
Incorrect or hesitant answers
Poor sterile/aseptic technique when distracted

stress_layers.description: Reduce skill time limit
Increase question speed and overlap
Another student plays family asking irrelevant questions
Interrupt mid-skill with a radio call requiring a one-sentence hospital update

objective: Reinforce weight-based pediatric medication dosing using the Broselow tape system
Build proficiency with 3-way stopcock technique for pediatric IV medication administration
Develop accuracy and speed in high-stress, low-frequency pediatric medication scenarios
Practice verbalized dosing confirmation (closed-loop: calculate, state dose aloud, confirm with partner before administration)
equipment: Broselow tape (current edition)
3-way stopcock assembly with IV tubing and flush syringe
Assorted syringes (1 mL, 3 mL, 5 mL, 10 mL)
Mock medication vials or labels (epinephrine 1:10,000, atropine, amiodarone, dextrose, normal saline flushes)
Pediatric weight/age scenario cards
Drug dosing reference card (for verification, not primary calculation)
Timer
Sharps-safe workspace
student_instructions: Student receives a pediatric scenario card with patient age, weight (or Broselow color), and the medication order

Student must: identify the correct Broselow color zone, calculate the correct dose using weight-based dosing, state the dose/concentration/volume aloud (closed-loop confirmation), draw the correct volume using appropriate syringe, demonstrate proper 3-way stopcock technique (connect, turn stopcock, administer, flush)
Time target: complete the full process within 3-5 minutes
Rotate through 2-3 medication scenarios per session

briefing.script: Pediatric medication errors are among the most dangerous things you can do in the field. Today's goal is accuracy first, then speed. Calculate, state your dose out loud, and confirm before you push anything.

during_station: Student works through: Broselow → calculation → draw → stopcock → administration
Observe silently during the attempt. Do not correct mid-attempt unless there is a critical safety error (10x dosing error)
debrief_each_student: Was the correct syringe size selected?
Was the 3-way stopcock technique correct (position, flush, no air)?
Did the student verbalize and confirm before pushing?

scenario_cards.calculation: 0.01 mg/kg × 12 kg = 0.12 mg. Concentration: 0.1 mg/mL (1:10,000). Volume: 0.12 / 0.1 = 1.2 mL
5 mg/kg × 18 kg = 90 mg. Concentration: 50 mg/mL. Volume: 90 / 50 = 1.8 mL
4 mL/kg × 8 kg = 32 mL of D10. (Alternatively: 0.5-1 g/kg dextrose = 4-8 g. D10 = 0.1 g/mL → 40-80 mL range.)

scenario_cards.expected_volume: 2 mL of 0.1 mg/mL dilution (or 4 mL of standard 0.1 mg/mL)
scenario_cards.calculation: 0.02 mg/kg × 20 kg = 0.4 mg. Minimum dose: 0.1 mg. Max single dose: 0.5 mg child.

scenario_cards.medication_order: Epinephrine 1:10,000 IV — second dose, cardiac arrest still in PEA
scenario_cards.calculation: 0.01 mg/kg × 14 kg = 0.14 mg. Same dose as first. Confirm ≥3 min since last dose.
scenario_cards.special_note: Must confirm timing ≥3 min from last dose before administering

scenario_cards.medication_order: Adenosine IV for SVT — first dose
scenario_cards.calculation: 0.1 mg/kg × 9 kg = 0.9 mg. Concentration: 3 mg/mL from vial → draw 0.3 mL OR dilute to 1 mg/mL first.
scenario_cards.special_note: MUST be rapid push with immediate NS flush via stopcock. Slow push = ineffective.

key_observations: Correct Broselow color identification
Accurate weight-based dose calculation (mg and mL)
Proper syringe selection for the volume
3-way stopcock positioning and flush technique
Verbal confirmation of dose before administration
Time to completion
common_errors: Confusing concentration with dose (mg vs mL)
Using the wrong Broselow zone (off by one color)
Drawing from the wrong concentration vial
Forgetting to flush the line after administration
Pushing adenosine slowly (must be rapid push + immediate flush)
Not verbalizing dose — just drawing and pushing silently

stress_layers.description: Reduce to 2 minutes
Background noise or panicked questions from parent
Give a second medication order immediately after the first
Provide incorrect weight and see if student catches it against Broselow

skill_drills.completion_criteria: Student successfully calculates correct dose, draws correct volume, demonstrates proper 3-way stopcock technique, and verbalizes confirmation for at least 2 of 3 scenario cards.

objective: Reinforce junctional wound packing technique for non-compressible hemorrhage sites (axilla, groin, neck)
Integrate hemorrhage control skills into a clinical context: hemorrhagic shock recognition, treatment sequencing, and transport decision-making
Build psychomotor confidence with hemostatic gauze application under pressure
equipment: Wound packing trainer (junctional wound model — commercial trainer or improvised with SimMan/moulage)
Hemostatic gauze (training rolls — QuikClot, Celox, or equivalent training material)
Combat gauze / standard gauze for comparison
Tourniquet (CAT or SOF-T — for extremity hemorrhage component if included)
Pressure dressing / Israeli bandage
IV/IO equipment (mock or training)
Blood pressure cuff, pulse oximeter, cardiac monitor
Moulage supplies: fake blood, wound inserts
Timer
student_instructions: Student receives a trauma scenario with a junctional hemorrhage that cannot be controlled by tourniquet

Student must: identify the hemorrhage source and recognize it as junctional (non-tourniquet-able), expose the wound appropriately, pack the wound with hemostatic gauze using correct technique (deep packing, direct pressure, maintain pressure 3+ min), apply a pressure dressing over the packed wound
Simultaneously manage hemorrhagic shock: assess mental status, vitals, IV/IO access, fluid resuscitation per protocol
Make transport decision (this is a load-and-go patient)
Time target: hemorrhage control initiated within 60 seconds of wound identification

briefing.script: Junctional hemorrhage kills fast and you can't tourniquet it. Today is about speed, technique, and managing the whole patient — not just the wound. Hemorrhage control is your first priority, but you still have a patient in shock who needs everything else too.

packing_technique_evaluation: Gauze packed deep into wound cavity (not laid on top)
Direct pressure maintained during and after packing
Hemostatic agent used appropriately (not just standard gauze for arterial junctional bleeding)
Pressure dressing applied securely over the packed wound
clinical_management_evaluation: Hemorrhagic shock recognized (tachycardia, hypotension, altered mental status)

expected_actions: Pack with hemostatic gauze deep into axillary wound

scenario_cards.complicating_factor: Active arterial bleeding — blood pooling rapidly
expected_actions: Immediate wound packing with hemostatic gauze

scenario_cards.complicating_factor: Expanding hematoma, patient anxious about breathing
expected_actions: Pack wound carefully — be aware of airway proximity

scenario_cards.complicating_factor: Impaled object removed by bystanders prior to arrival — active hemorrhage
expected_actions: Assess wound (impalement removed — now open hemorrhage)

scenario_cards.complicating_factor: PD on scene, patient in handcuffs, scene safety concerns
expected_actions: Request handcuff repositioning for access if safe
IO if IV difficult (patient in extremis)

key_observations: Hemorrhage source identified and junctional location recognized
Wound exposure (clothing removed/cut away appropriately)
Correct packing technique — deep, firm, using hemostatic agent
Pressure maintained for adequate time
Shock management initiated concurrently, not sequentially after wound packing
Transport decision appropriate for the clinical picture
common_errors: Laying gauze on top of wound instead of packing into cavity
Attempting tourniquet on a junctional wound
Releasing pressure too early to do other tasks
Tunnel vision on the wound while ignoring shock management
Delayed transport decision — spending too long on scene
Forgetting to reassess the wound/dressing for re-bleeding

stress_layers.description: Patient becomes combative from altered mental status
Dressing saturates through — student must re-pack
Second patient identified — forces triage decision
'Is he going to die?!'
Only one pack of gauze available

skill_drills.completion_criteria: Student demonstrates correct junctional wound packing technique and initiates concurrent hemorrhagic shock management within time targets.
